analogy: Think of "Popular Struggles and Movements" through the chapter's own opening images: O u t c o m e s o f D e m o c r a c y 63 Chapter 5 Outcomes of Democracy Overview As we begin to wind up our tour of democracy, it is time to move beyond our discussion of... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: Popular Struggles and Movements, Popular, Struggles, Movements. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Popular Struggles and Movements is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that o u t c o m e s o f D e m o c r a c y 63 Chapter 5 Outcomes of Democracy Overview As we begin to wind up our tour of democracy, it is time to move beyond our discussion of specific themes and ask a general set of questions: What does democracy do?

The text explains that or, what outcomes can we reasonably expect of democracy?

The text explains that also, does democracy fulfil these expectations in real life?

The text explains that we begin by thinking about how to assess the outcomes of democracy.

The text explains that after some clarity on how to think on this subject, we proceed to look at the expected and actual outcomes of democracy in various respects: quality of government, economic well-being, inequality, social differences and conflict and finally freedom and dignity.

The text explains that 64 D e m o c r a t i c P o l i t i c s How do we assess democracy’s outcomes?

The text explains that do you remember how students in Madam Lyngdoh’s class argued about democracy?

The text explains that it emerged from that conversation that democracy is a better form of government when compared with dictatorship or any other alternative.

The text explains that we felt that democracy was better because it:  Promotes equality among citizens;  Enhances the dignity of the individual;  Improves the quality of decision-making;  Provides a method to resolve conflicts; and  Allows room to correct mistakes.

The text explains that are these expectations realised under democracies?

The text explains that when we talk to people around us, most of them support democracy against other alternatives, such as rule by a monarch or military or religious leaders.

The text explains that but not so many of them would be satisfied with the democracy in practice.

The text explains that so we face a dilemma: democracy is seen to be good in principle, but felt to be not so good in its practice.

The text explains that this dilemma invites us to think hard about the outcomes of democracy.

The text explains that do we prefer democracy only for moral reasons?

The text explains that or are there some prudential reasons to support democracy too?

The text explains that over a hundred countries of the world today claim and practice some kind of democratic politics: they have formal constitutions, they hold elections, they have parties and they guarantee rights of citizens.

The text explains that while these features are common to most of them, these democracies are very much different from each other in terms of their social situations, their economic achievements and their cultures.

The text explains that the first step towards thinking carefully about the outcomes of democracy is to recognise that democracy is just a form of government.

The text explains that let us examine some of the things we can reasonably expect from democracy and examine the record of democracy.

The text explains that i loved that class because students were not being dictated any conclusions. © RK Laxman - Brushing up the years Is democracy all about coping with multiple pressures and accommodating diverse demands?

The text explains that o u t c o m e s o f D e m o c r a c y 65 Accountable, responsive and legitimate government There are some things that democracy must provide.

The text explains that in a democracy, we are most concerned with ensuring that people will have the right to choose their rulers and people will have control over the rulers.

The text explains that therefore, the most basic outcome of democracy should be that it produces a government that is accountable to the citizens, and responsive to the needs and expectations of the citizens.

The text explains that before we go into this question, we face another common question: Is the democratic government efficient?

The text explains that some people think that democracy produces less effective government.

The text explains that does that make democratic government inefficient?

The text explains that imagine a government that may take decisions very fast.

The text explains that in contrast, the democratic government will take more time to follow procedures before arriving at a decision.

The text explains that she has the right and the means to examine the process of decision making.

The text explains that this factor is often missing from a non-democratic government.

The text explains that therefore, when we are trying to find out the outcomes of democracy, it is right to expect democracy to produce a government that follows procedures and is accountable to the people.

The text explains that we can also expect that the democratic government develops mechanisms for citizens to hold the government accountable and mechanisms for citizens to take part in decision making whenever they think fit.

The text explains that can you think of what and how the government knows about you and your family (for example ration cards and voter identity cards)?

The text explains that what are the sources of information for you about the government?

The text explains that that is what we have discovered after all this mental gymnastics? major policies and legislations; and citizens’ right to information about the government and its functioning.

The text explains that democratic governments do not have a very good record when it comes to sharing information with citizens.

The text explains that in substantive terms, it may be reasonable to expect from democracy a government that is attentive to the needs and demands of the people and is largely free of corruption.

The text explains that there is one respect in which democratic government is certainly better than its alternatives: demo- cratic government is legitimate government.

The text explains that but a democratic government is people’s own government.

The text explains that source: SDSA Team, State of Democracy in South Asia, Delhi: Oxford University Press, 2007 Democracy is preferred over dictatorship everywhere except Pakistan South Asia 28 62 10 Those who agree with one of the statements Very few doubt the suitability of democracy for their own country How suitable is democracy for your country?

The text explains that o u t c o m e s o f D e m o c r a c y 67 Economic growth and development If democracies are expected to produce good governments, then is it not fair to expect that they would also produce development?

The text explains that but this alone cannot be reason to reject democracy.

The text explains that o u t c o m e s o f D e m o c r a c y 69 Reduction of inequality and poverty Perhaps more than development, it is reasonable to expect democracies to reduce economic disparities.

The text explains that parallel to the process of bringing individuals into the political arena on an equal footing, we find growing economic inequalities.

The text explains that those at the bottom of the society have very little to depend upon.

The text explains that he lives in Welcome Mazdoor Colony, a slum habitation in East Delhi.

The text explains that he lost his ration card and applied for a duplicate one in January 2004.

The text explains that he made several rounds to the local Food and Civil Supplies office for the next three months.

The text explains that but the clerks and officials would not even look at him, leave alone do his job or bother to tell him the status of his application.

The text explains that ultimately, he filed an application under the Right to Information Act asking for the daily progress made on his application, names of the officials, who were supposed to act on his application and what action would be taken against these officials for their inaction.

The text explains that within a week of filing application under the Right to Information Act, he was visited by an inspector from the Food Department, who informed him that the card had been made and he could collect it from the office.

Source: NCERT Class 10 Democratic Politics II, Chapter 5: Popular Struggles and Movements
